questions.text: Anti-venom given after a snake bite venom is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Artificial active immunity refers to the development of immunity through the administration of a vaccine or antigen. In this case, the anti-venom given after a snakebite is not an example of artificial active immunity, as it does not involve the administration of a vaccine.

Artificial passive immunity occurs when preformed antibodies are directly administered to an individual. In the case of a snakebite, anti-venom is administered to neutralize the effects of the snake venom. The antibodies in the anti-venom provide immediate protection and help to neutralize the venom toxins.

Natural active immunity refers to the development of immunity through natural exposure to an infectious agent. It occurs when the body's immune system recognizes and responds to a pathogen, leading to the production of specific antibodies. It is not applicable in the context of receiving anti-venom after a snakebite.

Natural passive immunity occurs when antibodies are transferred from a mother to her offspring, typically through breastfeeding or placental transfer. It provides temporary protection to the newborn until their own immune system develops. It is not relevant to the administration of anti-venom after a snakebite.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) Artificial passive immunity, as the administration of anti-venom provides immediate protection through the transfer of preformed antibodies. Artificially acquired passive immunity is protection acquired by giving a person an injection or transfusion of antibodies made by someone else. These antibodies neutralize the infectious agents in the usual way, but the protection lasts only a few weeks because the antibodies gradually break down and are not replaced.

questions.text: In an antibody molecule two heavy chains and two light chains are bonded by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Two light chains are connected to heavy chains by one disulphide bond each. Two heavy chains are connected by two disulphide bonds
Hydrogen bonds are not present in antibody molecule.
Phosphodiester bonds are not present in antibody molecule.
Ionic bonds are also absent in antibody molecule.

questions.explanation: An antibody molecule consists of four polypeptide chains—two identical light chain and two identical heavy chains—linked by disulfide (-S—S-) bridges

questions.text: Mucous membranes are part of body defense system and they offer:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mucous membranes are a type of epithelial tissue that lines the inside of many organs, including the respiratory tract, digestive tract, and genitourinary tract. They are part of the body's first line of defense against infection, and they offer physical barriers in several ways:The mucus layer: Mucus is a thick, sticky substance that is produced by the cells of the mucous membrane. It helps to trap bacteria and viruses, preventing them from reaching the underlying tissues.The tight junctions: The cells of the mucous membrane are held together by tight junctions, which are small gaps between the cells. These tight junctions help to prevent bacteria and viruses from passing between the cells.The cilia: The cells of the mucous membrane also have cilia, which are tiny hair-like projections. The cilia move in a coordinated fashion to help move the mucus layer along the surface of the mucous membrane. This helps to remove bacteria and viruses from the surface of the mucous membrane.
Mechanical barriers physically prevent pathogens from entering the body.
Chemical barriers kill or inhibit the growth of pathogens.

Biological barriers are made up of beneficial bacteria that live on the skin and in the mucous membranes. These bacteria help to prevent the growth of harmful bacteria by competing for nutrients and by producing antimicrobial substances.

questions.explanation: Mucous membranes act as physical barriers by trapping pathogens and preventing them from entering the body.

questions.text: T-lymphocytes become mature and competent under the influence of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Liver: The liver is an important organ in the body, but it is not involved in the maturation and competency of T-lymphocytes.
b) Bursa of Fabricius: The bursa of Fabricius is an organ found in birds that plays a role in the development and maturation of B-lymphocytes. It is not associated with T-lymphocyte maturation.

c) Thymus gland: This is the correct answer. T-lymphocytes, also known as T cells, mature and become competent in the thymus gland. The thymus is a primary lymphoid organ where T cells undergo differentiation and acquire their specific functions.
d) Spleen: The spleen is an organ that filters blood and plays a role in immune responses, but it is not directly involved in the maturation of T-lymphocytes.

questions.explanation: T-lymphocytes are a type of immune cells. They originate from stem cells in the bone marrow and are sent to the thymus gland for maturation which makes them immunocompetent.

questions.text: T-lymphocytes recognize antigens and attack microorganisms or transplanted organs and tissues. This effect is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T lymphocytes recognize an antigen and then attack microorganisms and the connection to it. Since this occurs cell to cell, it is called a cell-mediated response and opt a is right.

Humoral immunity is also called antibody-mediated immunity. With assistance from helper T cells, B cells will differentiate into plasma B cells that can produce antibodies against a specific antigen. The humoral immune system deals with antigens from pathogens that are freely circulating, or outside the infected cells.
The immunity which results from the production of antibodies by the immune system in response to the presence of an antigen is called Active Immunity.
The short-term immunity that results from the introduction of antibodies from another person or animal is called passive immunity.

questions.explanation: T lymphocytes recognize an antigen and then attack microorganisms and the connection to it. Since this occurs cell to cell, it is called a cell-mediated response and opt a is right.

questions.text: All are examples of 1st line of defense except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Skin is a barrier that serves as one of the body's first lines of defense against harmful microbes. Specialized immune cells within skin tissue help to fight invading organisms. Yet the skin hosts diverse communities of beneficial bacteria, collectively known as the skin microbiota.
The mucus covers the stomach wall with a protective coating.

The hydrochloric acid in the gastric juice breaks down the food and the digestive enzymes split up the proteins. The acidic gastric juice also kills bacteria. The mucus covers the stomach wall with a protective coating.

An antibody, also known as an immunoglobulin, is a large, Y-shaped protein used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects such as pathogenic bacteria and viruses. The antibody recognizes a unique molecule of the pathogen, called an antigen

questions.explanation: Skin, HCl, and mucous of barriers constitute 1st line of defense, while antibodies produced by lymphocytes, which belong to 3rd line of defense.

questions.text: The type of immunity that is present by birth is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The innate immune system is the body's first line of defense against germs entering the body. It responds in the same way to all germs and foreign substances, which is why it is sometimes referred to as the "nonspecific" immune system.

The adaptive immune system, also known as the acquired immune system, or specific immune system is a subsystem of the immune system that is composed of specialized, systemic cells and processes that eliminate pathogens or prevent their growth.

The adaptive immune system, also known as the acquired immune system, or specific immune system is a subsystem of the immune system that is composed of specialized, systemic cells and processes that eliminate pathogens or prevent their growth.

During the secondary immune response, the immune system can eliminate the antigen, which has been encountered by the individual during the primary invasion, more rapidly and efficiently. Both T and B memory cells contribute to the secondary response.

questions.explanation: The innate immunity, also known as the non-specific immunity or inborn immunity, is an important subsystem of the overall immune system that comprises the cells and mechanisms that defend the host from infection by other organisms.

questions.text: Stronger and specific immunity is found in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While many animals have immune systems that protect them from pathogens, the strength and specificity of immunity can vary widely across different animal species. Vertebrates generally have more advanced immune systems compared to invertebrates, but it's not consistent across all animals.

Humans possess a highly developed immune system, which includes both innate and adaptive immunity. Adaptive immunity, characterized by the presence of specialized immune cells like T cells and B cells, is specific and strong, providing targeted.

Invertebrates, which make up a vast majority of the animal kingdom, also have immune systems, but their immunity tends to be more primitive and less specific compared to vertebrates. Invertebrates rely on mechanisms like phagocytosis and antimicrobial peptides for defense, which are not as specific or potent as the adaptive immune responses found in vertebrates.

Vertebrates, including humans, possess a strong and specific immune system. Vertebrates have antibodies, T cells, and memory cells that allow for targeted responses to pathogens upon re-exposure. This specific immunity is a key characteristic of vertebrate immune systems. The correct answer is "Vertebrates." Vertebrates, including humans, possess a stronger and more specific immune system compared to invertebrates and most other animals, primarily due to the presence of adaptive immunity components like T and B cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is "Vertebrates." Vertebrates, including humans, possess a stronger and more specific immune system compared to invertebrates and most other animals, primarily due to the presence of adaptive immunity components like T and B cells.

questions.text: A vaccine contains:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Any foreign substance that induces an immune response in the body, especially by producing antibodies, is called an antigen. Vaccines have antigens (weakened, killed, or parts of a pathogen, like proteins). These antigens train the immune system to recognize and fight the real germ in the future.

Macrophages are specialised cells involved in the detection, phagocytosis and destruction of bacteria and other harmful organisms. In addition, they can also present antigens to T cells and initiate inflammation by releasing molecules (known as cytokines) that activate other cells

An antibody, also known as an immunoglobulin, is a large, Y-shaped protein used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects such as pathogenic bacteria and viruses. The antibody recognizes a unique molecule of the pathogen, called an antigen.
Only option A antigen is correct

questions.explanation: Any foreign substance that induces an immune response in the body, especially by producing antibodies, is called an antigen. Vaccines have antigens (weakened, killed, or parts of a pathogen, like proteins). These antigens train the immune system to recognize and fight the real germ in the future.

questions.text: B-lymphocytes originate in humans from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Blood cells are not responsible for the production of B lymphocytes.
The bursa of Fabricius is a structure found in birds where B lymphocytes were first discovered.
Bone marrow is responsible for the production of B lymphocytes in humans.
The thymus gland is responsible for the production and maturation of T lymphocytes.

questions.explanation: B-lymphocytes are cells of the immune system that are produced from bone marrow in humans, but in the case of birds, they originate from the Bursa of Fabricius, which is a lymphoid mass.

questions.text: Which one of the following glands is involved in the production of lymphocytes:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The main function of the pineal gland is to receive information about the state of the light-dark cycle from the environment and convey this information by the production and secretion of the hormone melatonin.

The organ's primary function is maturing T cells, or T lymphocytes. These are white blood cells responsible for fighting infections. Additionally, the thymus produces an array of hormones. Some of these, like thymulin and thymosin, regulate immune cell production.

It regulates growth, metabolism, and reproduction through the hormones that it produces. The production of these hormones is either stimulated or inhibited by chemical messages sent from the hypothalamus to the pituitary. The posterior lobe produces two hormones, vasopressin and oxytocin.

Adrenal glands, also known as suprarenal glands, are small, triangular-shaped glands located on top of both kidneys. Adrenal glands produce hormones that help regulate your metabolism, immune system, blood pressure, response to stress and other essential functions.

questions.explanation: The thymus is a specialized primary lymphoid organ of the immune system. Within the thymus, lymphocytes or T cells mature. T cells are critical to the adaptive immune system, where the body adapts specifically to foreign invaders.

questions.text: T-lymphocytcs recognize antigen and attack microorganisms or transplanted organ and tissues. The effect is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity or cellular immunity is an immune response that does not involve antibodies. Rather, cell-mediated immunity is the activation of phagocytes, antigen-specific cytotoxic T-lymphocytes, and the release of various cytokines in response to an antigen.

Active Immunity results when exposure to a disease organism triggers the immune system to produce antibodies to that disease. Active immunity can be acquired through natural immunity or vaccine-induced immunity.

Humoral immunity is the aspect of immunity that is mediated by macromolecules - including secreted antibodies, complement proteins, and certain antimicrobial peptides - located in extracellular fluids. Humoral immunity is named so because it involves substances found in the humors, or body fluids.

Passive immunity is provided when a person is given antibodies to a disease rather than producing them through his or her own immune system. A newborn baby acquires passive immunity from its mother through the placenta.

questions.explanation: The humoral immune system deals with antigens from pathogen, that are freely circulating, or outside the infected cells. Cellular immunity occurs inside infected cells and is mediated by T lymphocytes.

questions.text: Lymphocytes' function is to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Macrophages are usually involved in engulfing bacteria and phagocytosis.
These are not the functions of lymphocytes.
Antibodies are produced by lymphocytes.
Blood clotting is usually initiated by heparin protein presence in blood.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are produced by plasma cells of B-lymphocytes.

questions.text: In which response, B-cells produce plasma cells that synthesize antibodies and release them into the blood plasma and tissue fluids?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity or cellular immunity is an immune response that does not involve antibodies. Rather, cell-mediated immunity is the activation of phagocytes, antigen-specific cytotoxic T-lymphocytes, and the release of various cytokines in response to an antigen.

Humoral immunity is the aspect of immunity that is mediated by macromolecules - including secreted antibodies, complement proteins, and certain antimicrobial peptides - located in extracellular fluids. Humoral immunity is named so because it involves substances found in the humors, or body fluids
The hormonal responses bring about or aid in inducing cardiovascular adjustments, energy metabolism, thermoregulations, and immunity changes during exercise.

Phototactic responses are observed in a number of bacteria and archae, such as Serratia marcescens. Photoreceptor proteins are light-sensitive proteins involved in the sensing and response to light in a variety of organisms. Some examples are bacteriorhodopsin and bacteriophytochromes in some bacteria.

questions.explanation: Humoral immunity is the aspect of immunity that is mediated by macromolecules found in extracellular fluids, such as secreted antibodies, complement proteins, and certain antimicrobial peptides. Humoral immunity is named so because it involves substances found in the humors, or body fluids.

questions.text: To combat the active infections of tetanus, rabies, and snakes, the _ method of immunization is used.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This method involves administering weakened or killed pathogens (vaccines) to stimulate the body's immune system to produce its antibodies, which takes time to develop protection and is not suitable for immediate needs like active infections.

This term is not a standard immunization method. The humoral immune response involves the production of antibodies by B cells to fight infections, but it does not refer to a specific immunization approach.
While "active" refers to the body's immune response, "artificial" is not a standard term for immunization methods. The correct term is "active immunization."
Passive immunization is used to combat the active infections of tetanus, rabies, and snake bites.

questions.explanation: Passive immunity can also be transferred artificially by introducing antibodies with the help of vaccinations derived from animals or human beings who are already immune to that disease.

questions.text: Vaccination is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Natural immunity is acquired from exposure to the disease organism through infection with the actual disease. Vaccine-induced immunity is acquired through the introduction of a killed or weakened form of the disease organism through vaccination

Active artificially acquired immunity refers to any immunization with an antigen. During artificially acquired active immunity, one is immunized with one or more of the following: attenuated microbes, killed organisms, fragmented microorganisms, or antigens produced by recombinant DNA technology, or toxoids.

Passive immunity provides immediate but short-lived protection, lasting several weeks up to 3 or 4 months. Passive immunity can occur naturally, when maternal antibodies are transferred to the fetus through the placenta or from breast milk to the gut of the infant.

Artificially-acquired passive immunity is an immediate, but short-term immunization provided by the injection of antibodies, such as gamma globulin, that are not produced by the recipient's cells. These antibodies are developed in another individual or animal and then injected into another individual.

questions.explanation: Active artificially acquired immunity refers to any immunization with an antigen. During artificially acquired active immunity, one is immunized with one or more of the following: attenuated microbes, killed organisms, fragmented microorganisms, or antigens produced by recombinant DNA technology, or toxoids.

questions.text: Cell mediated immune response is given by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neutrophils are a type of white blood cell that plays a critical role in innate immunity and inflammation. They are among the first cells to arrive at the site of infection or tissue damage. Neutrophils are primarily involved in phagocytosis and the destruction of pathogens. However, they are not the primary cells involved in cell-mediated immune responses.

Macrophages are immune cells that engulf and destroy foreign substances, dead cells, and pathogens. They are an important component of the immune system and are involved in both innate and adaptive immune responses. Macrophages play a crucial role in antigen presentation and the activation of other immune cells, including T-lymphocytes. They are involved in both cell-mediated and humoral immune responses.

The correct answer is C) T-lymphocytes, also known as T cells. T-lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that plays a central role in cell-mediated immune responses. They are involved in recognizing and attacking specific pathogens or infected cells. T-lymphocytes are responsible for cell-mediated immunity, which includes the activation of other immune cells, killing infected cells, and regulating the immune response.

B-lymphocytes, or B cells, are another type of white blood cell involved in the immune response. However, they primarily participate in humoral immunity, which involves the production of antibodies by B cells. B-lymphocytes are responsible for recognizing and binding to specific antigens, leading to the production of antibodies that help neutralize pathogens. They are not the primary cells involved in cell-mediated immune responses.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is C) T-lymphocytes, T cells are a diverse and important group of lymphocytes that mature and undergo positive and negative selection processes in the thymus. These cells play a vital role in both components of active immunity, including cell-mediated and to some extent humoral immunity.

questions.text: The prevention of disease by artificial activation of immune response is possible by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gene therapy is a technique that involves the manipulation of genes to treat or prevent diseases. While gene therapy has the potential to treat certain diseases, it is not primarily focused on the artificial activation of the immune response. Gene therapy aims to correct genetic defects or introduce therapeutic genes into the cells.

Drugs can be used for disease treatment, but they are not typically used for the artificial activation of the immune response. Drugs may include medications that target specific pathogens, inhibit their growth, or modulate the immune response. However, the prevention of disease through the artificial activation of the immune response is usually achieved through other means.

Vaccines are biological preparations that stimulate the immune system to recognize and mount an immune response against specific pathogens or antigens. Vaccines can prevent diseases by introducing harmless components or weakened forms of the pathogen, which trigger an immune response and provide immunity against future infections.

Antibodies are proteins produced by the immune system in response to specific antigens. While antibodies play a critical role in the immune response, they are not used for the artificial activation of the immune response. Antibodies can be administered passively as a form of immunotherapy, but they do not directly activate the immune response.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is C) Vaccines, as they are specifically designed to prevent diseases by artificially activating the immune response and generating immunity against specific pathogens or antigens.

questions.text: The most active phagocytic white blood cells are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, eosinophils and lymphocytes are not the most active phagocytic white blood cells. Eosinophils are less abundant than neutrophils. Eosinophils are involved in allergic reactions and parasitic infections. Lymphocytes are not as active phagocytic white blood cells as neutrophils and eosinophils. However, lymphocytes are important for the immune system because they produce antibodies, which help the body to fight off infection.

Neutrophils and monocytes are the most active phagocytic white blood cells in the human body. Phagocytosis is the process of engulfing and destroying foreign particles, such as bacteria and viruses. Neutrophils and monocytes are both professional phagocytes, meaning that they are specialized in this process. Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the blood. They are the first line of defense against infection, and they are quickly recruited to the site of infection. Neutrophils can engulf and destroy bacteria within minutes of entering the bloodstream.Monocytes are larger than neutrophils and have a longer lifespan. They circulate in the blood for a few days before migrating to tissues, where they become macrophages. Macrophages are long-lived cells that are able to phagocytose large amounts of material. They are important for clearing up debris and dead cells after an infection.

Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the blood. They are the first line of defense against infection, and they are quickly recruited to the site of infection. Neutrophils can engulf and destroy bacteria within minutes of entering the bloodstream. Eosinophils are another type of phagocyte. They are not as abundant as neutrophils, but they are more specialized for fighting parasites. Eosinophils are also involved in allergic reactions.

Lymphocytes are not the most active phagocytic white blood cells. Macrophages are the most active phagocytic white blood cells. Lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that is responsible for the body's adaptive immune response. They are produced in the bone marrow and lymph nodes. Lymphocytes can be divided into two main types: B cells and T cells. B cells produce antibodies, which are proteins that bind to and neutralize foreign invaders. T cells help to destroy infected cells and cells that are not recognized by the immune system. Macrophages: Macrophages are a type of white blood cell that is responsible for the body's innate immune response. They are produced in the bone marrow and are found in tissues throughout the body. Macrophages are phagocytic, meaning that they can engulf and destroy foreign invaders.

questions.explanation: Neutrophils and monocytes are the most active phagocytic white blood cells in the human body. Phagocytosis is the process of engulfing and destroying foreign particles, such as bacteria and viruses. Neutrophils and monocytes are both professional phagocytes, meaning that they are specialized in this process. Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the blood. They are the first line of defense against infection, and they are quickly recruited to the site of infection. Neutrophils can engulf and destroy bacteria within minutes of entering the bloodstream.Monocytes are larger than neutrophils and have a longer lifespan. They circulate in the blood for a few days before migrating to tissues, where they become macrophages. Macrophages are long-lived cells that are able to phagocytose large amounts of material. They are important for clearing up debris and dead cells after an infection.

questions.text: Which one of the following gives rise to macrophages?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neutrophils have a very short life span and only initiate the reaction by direct destruction of pathogen, hence they can not give rise to macrophages.

Eosinophils have a rather longer life span and they get attracted to places where there is inflammation, hence large amounts of it can be shown in a blood test and allow us to know that there is an infection.

Monocytes have a rather longer life span and take a more intensive approach to killing the pathogen compared to the neutrophils. They are also able to give rise to macrophages, hence it is the correct option.

There are 2 types of lymphocytes, B and T lymphocytes. They both are made in the bone marrow, but B lymphocyte matures in it whereas T lymphocyte matures in the thymus gland. They both are white blood cells and they kill the pathogen by attacking it with the help of its killer cells. They do not give rise to macrophages, therefore it is a wrong option.

questions.explanation: Monocytes have a relatively longer lifespan and employ a more intensive approach to killing pathogens compared to neutrophils. They are also able to give rise to macrophages, hence it is the correct option.

questions.text: The capacity of our body to identify and eradicate intruders is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect because disinfection is the physical or chemical process to destroy or remove small undesirable animal forms, particularly arthropods or rodents, present upon the person, clothing, or environment of an individual or domestic animals.

Incorrect because chemotherapy is a drug treatment that uses powerful chemicals to kill fast-growing cells in your body. Chemotherapy is most often used to treat cancer, since cancer cells grow and multiply much more quickly than most cells in the body.
Incorrect because antisepsis is the method of using chemicals, called antiseptics, to destroy the germs that cause infections.

In biology, immunity is the state of being insusceptible or resistant to a noxious agent or process, especially a pathogen or infectious disease. Immunity may occur naturally or be produced by prior exposure or immunization.

questions.explanation: In biology, immunity is the balanced state of multicellular organisms having adequate biological defenses to fight infection, diseases or other unwanted biological invasions, while having adequate tolerance to avoid allergy and autoimmune diseases.

questions.text: Pick the one which is not role of plasma cells:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasma cells are responsible for the synthesis of antibodies, which are crucial in the immune response.

options.text: Liberation of antibodies into the blood plasma
options.explanation: Plasma cells release antibodies into the bloodstream to help neutralize pathogens.

options.text: Attaching the antibodies to the surface of bacteria
options.explanation: Plasma cells do not directly attach antibodies to bacteria; this process occurs through the action of antibodies themselves binding to antigens on bacterial surfaces.

options.text: Liberation of antibodies into tissue fluid
options.explanation: Plasma cells also release antibodies into tissue fluid, aiding in the immune defense.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are synthesized and liberated into the blood by plasma cells.However, they do not attach them. Antibodies are attached themselves, to the surface of bacteria

questions.text: Louis Pasteur used the word vaccine for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cowpox pus was used by Edward Jenner, not Louis Pasteur. Jenner's work led to the development of the smallpox vaccine.
This is correct. Louis Pasteur used the term 'vaccine' to describe attenuated cultures of bacteria, which he used for immunization.
Smallpox pus is related to Edward Jenner's work, not Pasteur's. Jenner used cowpox material to vaccinate against smallpox.
While Pasteur did work with attenuated cultures, the term 'vaccine' was specifically used for bacterial cultures initially. The use of vaccines for viruses developed later.

questions.explanation: Louis Pasteur called the attenuated cultures of bacterial vaccine and immunization with attenuated cultures of bacteria vaccination

questions.text: A typical antibody molecule is shaped:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It's made of two heavy chains and two light chains, joined by disulfide bonds, with the arms of the "Y" binding to specific antigens.
An antibody has two identical antigen-binding sites at the tips of the "Y," formed by the variable regions of its heavy and light chains.
Incorrect. The "J" term in immunology usually refers to the J (joining) chain found in certain antibodies like IgA and IgM, which links multiple antibody units together.
A standard antibody molecule is Y-shaped; not H.

questions.explanation: Each antibody consists of four polypeptides–two heavy chains and two light chains joined to form a “Y” shaped molecule.

questions.text: Antibodies are proteins and made up of how many polypeptide chains
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect because an antibody is composed of 4 polypeptide chains, not one.
This is also incorrect because antibodies are composed of 4 polypeptide chains.
This is incorrect because antibodies are made of 4 polypeptide chains; 2 heavy chains and 2 light chains.
Antibodies are immune system-related proteins called immunoglobulins. Each antibody consists of four polypeptide chains 2 heavy chains and 2 light chains joined together to form Y-shaped molecule.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are immune system-related proteins called immunoglobulins. Each antibody consists of four polypeptide chains 2 heavy chains and 2 light chains joined together to form Y Y-shaped molecule. Hence opt D is the right choice here. Each antibody has a variable region and a constant region. The variable region is composed of 110-130 amino acids which gives the antibody its specificity to bind to antigen. The variable region consists of the end of both light and heavy chains.

questions.text: A typical antibody molecule is made up of how many identical light chains?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An antibody does not have only one light chain; it is structured with pairs of chains for stability and function.
This is correct. A typical antibody molecule consists of two identical light chains and two heavy chains.
An antibody does not have three light chains; it is always in pairs of two light chains.
An antibody does have four chains in total, but not four light chains. It consists of two light chains and two heavy chains.

questions.explanation: Each antibody consists of four polypeptides–two heavy chains and two light chains

questions.text: Which part of the antibody recognizes the antigen during immune response?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Heavy part: The heavy chains contribute to the overall structure of the antibody, but do not directly participate in antigen recognition.
Light part: Similar to the heavy chains, the light chains contribute to the antibody's structure but do not have the variable regions necessary for antigen binding.
Constant part: This region is the same for all antibodies of a particular class and is involved in effector functions (like activating the complement system) rather than antigen recognition.

Antibodies are immune system-related proteins called immunoglobulins. Each antibody consists of four polypeptide chains 2 heavy chains and 2 light chains joined together to form Y Y-shaped molecule. Each antibody has a variable region and a constant region. The variable region is composed of 110-130 amino acids which gives the antibody its specificity to bind to antigen. The variable region consists of the end of both light and heavy chains.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are immune system-related proteins called immunoglobulins. Each antibody consists of four polypeptide chains 2 heavy chains and 2 light chains joined together to form Y Y-shaped molecule. Each antibody has a variable region and a constant region. The variable region is composed of 110-130 amino acids which gives the antibody its specificity to bind to antigen. The variable region consists of the end of both light and heavy chains.

questions.text: In an antibody molecule two heavy chains and two light chains are bonded by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Two light chains are connected to heavy chains by one disulphide bond each. Two heavy chains are connected by two disulphide bonds
Hydrogen bonds are not present in antibody molecule.
Phosphodiester bonds are not present in antibody molecule.
Ionic bonds are also absent in antibody molecule.

questions.explanation: An antibody molecule consists of four polypeptide chains—two identical light chain and two identical heavy chains—linked by disulfide (-S—S-) bridges

questions.text: Substance that can be recognized by the receptor of B-cells:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An antigen is a molecule that is recognized by the immune system, specifically by the receptors on B-cells, which triggers an immune response, including the production of antibodies. These can be proteins, polysaccharides, or other molecules from pathogens like bacteria and viruses.

Antibodies are proteins produced by B-cells in response to antigens. They bind to antigens to neutralize or mark them for destruction by other immune cells. Antibodies themselves are not recognized substances; they are the agents that recognize antigens.

An immunogen is any substance that elicits an immune response. While all antigens are immunogens, not all immunogens are necessarily antigens recognized by B-cell receptors. Immunogens include substances that stimulate both B-cells and T-cells.

Food is typically not recognized as a threat by the immune system unless it contains allergens that can act as antigens. Generally, food is not recognized by B-cell receptors in the context of an immune response.

questions.explanation: An antigen is a molecule that is recognized by the immune system, specifically by the receptors on B-cells, which triggers an immune response, including the production of antibodies. These can be proteins, polysaccharides, or other molecules from pathogens like bacteria and viruses.

questions.text: The study of our protection from foreign macromolecules or invading organisms and our responses to them is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bacteriology is the branch of science concerned with the study of bacteria. It does not encompass the study of the immune system's response to invaders.
Virology is the study of viruses and virus-like agents. While it involves studying entities that can invade the body, it does not focus on the body's defense mechanisms.
Ethology is the study of animal behavior, not related to the immune responses or protection from foreign invaders.
Immunology is the branch of biomedical science that deals with the study of the immune system, including the body's defense mechanisms against foreign macromolecules and invading organisms.

questions.explanation: The study of our protection from foreign macro molecules or invading organisms and our responses to them is called immunology.

questions.text: The body's response to foreign particles, such as the production of antibodies directed against a specific antigen, is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An immune response is the body's collective reaction to the presence of foreign substances, such as pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi), allergens, or other antigens (molecules that the immune system recognizes as foreign). The immune response is a complex and highly coordinated series of actions by the immune system to identify, neutralize, and eliminate these foreign substances in order to maintain the body's health and protect it from infections and diseases.

Immunity, in the context of the immune system and human health, refers to the body's ability to resist and defend itself against foreign substances, such as pathogens (like bacteria, viruses, and fungi) and harmful foreign molecules (antigens). Immunity is a crucial aspect of the immune system's function and plays a central role in maintaining overall health and protecting the body from infections and diseases.

In the human body, temperature response refers to the various ways in which the body regulates and adapts to changes in temperature to maintain its core temperature within a narrow and optimal range for normal physiological function. This process is known as thermoregulation and is essential for overall health and survival.

The inflammatory response is a fundamental and complex biological process that occurs in the body as a reaction to various types of injuries, infections, and irritants. It is a key component of the immune system and serves as a protective mechanism to remove harmful stimuli, initiate the healing process, and restore tissue homeostasis.

questions.explanation: An immune response is the body's collective reaction to the presence of foreign substances, such as pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi), allergens, or other antigens (molecules that the immune system recognizes as foreign). The immune response is a complex and highly coordinated series of actions by the immune system to identify, neutralize, and eliminate these foreign substances in order to maintain the body's health and protect it from infections and diseases. The body's response to foreign particles, such as the production of antibodies directed against a specific antigen, is called immune response.

questions.text: The antibodies are transported through_and the_to the pathogen invasion sites:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct. Antibodies are primarily transported through the bloodstream and the lymphatic system to reach sites of infection.
Incorrect. Water and floods are not biological systems used to transport antibodies in the human body.
Incorrect. While injections can introduce antibodies into the body, they are not natural transport systems within the body for antibodies.
Incorrect. Food does not transport antibodies, and injections are not a natural transport method within the body.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are primarily transported through the bloodstream and the lymphatic system to reach sites of infection. The antibodies are transported through blood and the lymph to the pathogen invasion sites.

questions.text: Common feature of cell humoral response and cell mediated response are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Recognition of antigen happens in both responses, but by different lymphocytes (T-cells in cell-mediated, B-cells in humoral).
Tissue rejection is mainly a cell-mediated immune response (cytotoxic T-cells)
Production of antibodies only occurs in humoral immunity (by plasma B-cells).
Plasma clone formation is specific to humoral immunity (B-cell proliferation).

questions.explanation: The common feature between cell-mediated response (T-cells) and humoral response (B-cells) is recognition of antigen.

questions.text: Antibodies are injected in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Active artificially acquired immunity refers to any immunization with an antigen. During artificially acquired active immunity, one is immunized with one or more of the following: attenuated microbes, killed organisms, fragmented microorganisms, or antigens produced by recombinant DNA technology, or toxoids

Natural immunity is acquired from exposure to the disease organism through infection with the actual disease. Vaccine-induced immunity is acquired through the introduction of a killed or weakened form of the disease organism through vaccination.

Artificially-acquired passive immunity is an immediate, but short-term immunization provided by the injection of antibodies, such as gamma globulin, that are not produced by the recipient's cells. These antibodies are developed in another individual or animal and then injected into another individual.

Passive immunity provides immediate but short-lived protection, lasting several weeks up to 3 or 4 months. Passive immunity can occur naturally, when maternal antibodies are transferred to the fetus through the placenta or from breast milk to the gut of the infant.

questions.explanation: Artificially-acquired passive immunity is an immediate, but short-term immunization provided by the injection of antibodies, such as gamma globulin, that are not produced by the recipient's cells. These antibodies are developed in another individual or animal and then injected into another individual.

questions.text: Any foreign body that may activate immune system:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An antigen is any substance that causes your immune system to produce antibodies against it. This means your immune system does not recognize the substance, and is trying to fight it off. An antigen may be a substance from the environment, such as chemicals, bacteria, viruses, or pollen.

An antibody, also known as an immunoglobulin, is a large, Y-shaped protein used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects such as pathogenic bacteria and viruses. The antibody recognizes a unique molecule of the pathogen, called an antigen.

An antibody, also known as an immunoglobulin, is a large, Y-shaped protein used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects such as pathogenic bacteria and viruses. The antibody recognizes a unique molecule of the pathogen, called an antigen.
It is any of a group of phospholipids in which a fatty acid group is replaced by a fatty aldehyde and which include lecithins, phosphatidylethanolamines, and phosphatidylserines.

questions.explanation: An antigen is any substance that causes your immune system to produce antibodies against it. This means your immune system does not recognize the substance, and is trying to fight it off. An antigen may be a substance from the environment, such as chemicals, bacteria, viruses, or pollen.

questions.text: Immunity is generally destroyed in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All infections / inflammations / diseases do not generally destroy immunity; some may temporarily weaken it, but HIV is unique in progressively dismantling it.

Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is an infection that attacks the body’s immune system. Acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) is the most advanced stage of the disease. HIV targets the body’s white blood cells, weakening the immune system. This makes it easier to get sick with diseases like tuberculosis, infections and some cancers.HIV is spread from the body fluids of an infected person, including blood, breast milk, semen and vaginal fluids. It is not spread by kisses, hugs or sharing food. It can also spread from a mother to her baby.HIV can be treated and prevented with antiretroviral therapy (ART). Untreated HIV can progress to AIDS, often after many years.
All infections / inflammations / diseases do not generally destroy immunity; some may temporarily weaken it, but HIV is unique in progressively dismantling it.
All infections / inflammations / diseases do not generally destroy immunity; some may temporarily weaken it, but HIV is unique in progressively dismantling it.

questions.explanation: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is an infection that attacks the body’s immune system. Acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) is the most advanced stage of the disease. HIV targets the body’s white blood cells, weakening the immune system. This makes it easier to get sick with diseases like tuberculosis, infections and some cancers.HIV is spread from the body fluids of an infected person, including blood, breast milk, semen and vaginal fluids. It is not spread by kisses, hugs or sharing food. It can also spread from a mother to her baby.HIV can be treated and prevented with antiretroviral therapy (ART). Untreated HIV can progress to AIDS, often after many years.

questions.text: Phagocytosis usually involves:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The innate immune system is the body's first line of defense against germs entering the body. It responds in the same way to all germs and foreign substances, which is why it is sometimes referred to as the "nonspecific" immune system.

The third line of defense is immune cells that target specific antigens. The immune cells that play a role in the third line of defense are B-cells and T-cells, both are white blood cells. The B-cells produce antibodies. The T-cells help identify pathogenic cells and destroy targeted cells.

The second line of defense is nonspecific resistance that destroys invaders in a generalized way without targeting specific individuals: Phagocytic cells ingest and destroy all microbes that pass into body tissues. For example macrophages are cells derived from monocytes (a type of white blood cell).
There are only three major lines of defenses

questions.explanation: Phagocytes are type of cells within the body capable of engulfing and absorbing bacteria and other small cells or particles. These are neutrophils and monocytes that form 2nd line of defense.

questions.text: Cyclosporine is used to inhibit which type of response?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity or cellular immunity is an immune response that does not involve antibodies. Rather, cell-mediated immunity is the activation of phagocytes, antigen-specific cytotoxic T-lymphocytes, and the release of various cytokines in response to an antigen.

Humoral immunity is the aspect of immunity that is mediated by macromolecules - including secreted antibodies, complement proteins, and certain antimicrobial peptides - located in extracellular fluids. Humoral immunity is named so because it involves substances found in the humors, or body fluids.
It doesn't respond to cell signaling
Cyclosporine doesn't respond by cell-to-cell mechanism

questions.explanation: Cyclosporine is used to prevent organ rejection (cell mediated) in people who have received a liver, kidney or, heart transplant. It is usually taken along with other medications to allow your new organ to function normally . Cyclosporine belongs to a class of drugs known as immuno-suppressants.

questions.text: All are true about passive immunization except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While antibiotics are essential for treating bacterial infections, they do not provide immunity. They are not part of passive immunization, which involves antibodies.
Antisera, which are preparations containing antibodies from another individual or animal, are indeed used in passive immunization to provide immediate, but temporary, immunity.
Antitoxins can be considered a form of passive immunization as they provide immediate immunity against toxins, but they are not commonly referred to as passive immunization in all contexts.
This statement is false because in passive immunization, the antibodies are provided from an external source rather than produced by the individual's own immune system.

questions.explanation: Passive immunization involves the administration of pre-formed antibodies to an individual, providing immediate but short-lived protection against infections. This process does not involve the individual's immune system actively producing antibodies. Therefore, the statement about producing antibodies is false. The other options, while potentially misinterpreted, relate to the administration of substances meant for therapeutic immunity, but they do not directly state that the body is producing its own antibodies.

questions.text: Antibodies are proteins and made up of how many polypeptide chains
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect because an antibody is composed of 4 polypeptide chains, not one.
This is also incorrect because antibodies are composed of 4 polypeptide chains.
This is incorrect because antibodies are made of 4 polypeptide chains; 2 heavy chains and 2 light chains.
Antibodies are immune system-related proteins called immunoglobulins. Each antibody consists of four polypeptide chains 2 heavy chains and 2 light chains joined together to form Y-shaped molecule.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are immune system-related proteins called immunoglobulins. Each antibody consists of four polypeptide chains 2 heavy chains and 2 light chains joined together to form Y Y-shaped molecule. Hence opt D is the right choice here. Each antibody has a variable region and a constant region. The variable region is composed of 110-130 amino acids which gives the antibody its specificity to bind to antigen. The variable region consists of the end of both light and heavy chains.

questions.text: The type of immunity in which antibodies are passed from one individual to another is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Passive immunity is provided when a person is given antibodies to a disease rather than producing them through his or her own immune system. A newborn baby acquires passive immunity from its mother through the placenta.

Natural immunity is acquired from exposure to the disease organism through infection with the actual disease. Vaccine-induced immunity is acquired through the introduction of a killed or weakened form of the disease organism through vaccination.

Active artificially acquired immunity refers to any immunization with an antigen. During artificially acquired active immunity, one is immunized with one or more of the following: attenuated microbes, killed organisms, fragmented microorganisms, or antigens produced by recombinant DNA technology, or toxoids.

Humoral immunity is the aspect of immunity that is mediated by macromolecules - including secreted antibodies, complement proteins, and certain antimicrobial peptides - located in extracellular fluids. Humoral immunity is named so because it involves substances found in the humors, or body fluids.

questions.explanation: Passive immunity is given from mother to child through the placenta before birth, and through breast milk after birth. It can also be given medically through blood products that contain antibodies, such as immune globulin. This type of immunity is fast-acting but lasts only a few weeks or months.

questions.text: ln immunoglobulins/antibodies, two light chains and two heavy chains are linked to each other by?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antibodies could also have non-covalent interactions for antigen binding, but the specific link between heavy and light chains is through disulfide covalent bonds, not generic covalent bonds.
Disulphide bonds are responsible for the link between two heavy chains and also between light and he chains
They help stabilize protein folding and antigen–antibody binding, but they do not hold the heavy and light chains together.
They may contribute to the tertiary structure stability, but again, they do not serve as the main linkage between antibody chains.

questions.explanation: An antibody, also known as an immunoglobulin, is a large, Y-shaped protein used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects such as pathogenic bacteria and viruses. The antibody recognizes a unique molecule of the pathogen, called an antigen.Explanation is given below:

questions.text: Anti-venom given after a snake bite venom is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Artificial active immunity refers to the development of immunity through the administration of a vaccine or antigen. In this case, the anti-venom given after a snakebite is not an example of artificial active immunity, as it does not involve the administration of a vaccine.

Artificial passive immunity occurs when preformed antibodies are directly administered to an individual. In the case of a snakebite, anti-venom is administered to neutralize the effects of the snake venom. The antibodies in the anti-venom provide immediate protection and help to neutralize the venom toxins.

Natural active immunity refers to the development of immunity through natural exposure to an infectious agent. It occurs when the body's immune system recognizes and responds to a pathogen, leading to the production of specific antibodies. It is not applicable in the context of receiving anti-venom after a snakebite.

Natural passive immunity occurs when antibodies are transferred from a mother to her offspring, typically through breastfeeding or placental transfer. It provides temporary protection to the newborn until their own immune system develops. It is not relevant to the administration of anti-venom after a snakebite.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) Artificial passive immunity, as the administration of anti-venom provides immediate protection through the transfer of preformed antibodies. Artificially acquired passive immunity is protection acquired by giving a person an injection or transfusion of antibodies made by someone else. These antibodies neutralize the infectious agents in the usual way, but the protection lasts only a few weeks because the antibodies gradually break down and are not replaced.

questions.text: Is a foreign substance, which stimulates the formation of antibodies.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antibiotics are medications used to treat bacterial infections by inhibiting the growth or killing bacteria. They are not foreign substances that stimulate the formation of antibodies. Antibiotics do not directly stimulate the immune system or the formation of antibodies.

Antigens are foreign substances that can stimulate the immune system to produce an immune response, including the formation of antibodies. Antigens can be components of pathogens, such as proteins or carbohydrates on the surface of bacteria or viruses, or they can be molecules from other sources that trigger an immune response.

Archaea are a distinct domain of single-celled microorganisms. While they are interesting in terms of evolution and diversity, they are not directly related to the formation of antibodies or the immune response.

Transposons, also known as jumping genes, are DNA sequences that can move or transpose within the genome of an organism. They have implications in genetic variation and evolution but are not directly involved in the formation of antibodies.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) Antigens, as they are foreign substances that stimulate the immune system and lead to the formation of antibodies.

questions.text: Antigen is a foreign protein or any other molecule which stimulates the formation of :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) MHC complex: Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) molecules are proteins found on the surface of cells. They play a crucial role in the immune system by presenting antigens to T cells, which are a type of immune cell. MHC complexes help T cells recognize antigens, but they are not the molecules that stimulate the formation of antibodies.

b) Immunogen: An immunogen is a substance, such as a foreign protein or other molecule, that stimulates the immune system to produce an immune response. When an immunogen enters the body, it triggers the production of antibodies and activates other components of the immune system to fight off the foreign substance.

c) Mucus: Mucus is a thick, slimy substance produced by mucous membranes in the body. It serves as a protective barrier in various organs, including the respiratory and digestive tracts, but it does not stimulate the formation of antibodies.

d) Antibodies: Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of antigens. Antibodies specifically bind to antigens, marking them for destruction by immune cells. While antibodies are produced in response to antigens, they are not responsible for stimulating their own formation.

questions.explanation: d) Antibodies: Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of antigens. Antibodies specifically bind to antigens, marking them for destruction by immune cells. While antibodies are produced in response to antigens, they are not responsible for stimulating their own formation.

questions.text: In the diagram below which of the following cells is responsible for generating antibodies ?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neutrophils are a type of white blood cell that respond rapidly to infection, primarily targeting bacteria and fungi. They do not generate antibodies, but rather they are involved in the initial defense against pathogens through phagocytosis and the release of enzymes.

Monocytes are another form of white blood cell that differentiate into macrophages and dendritic cells. While they play a role in the immune response and can present antigens, they do not produce antibodies directly.

B-Lymphocytes, or B cells, are crucial for the adaptive immune response as they are responsible for the production of antibodies. When activated, they can differentiate into plasma cells that secrete antibodies specific to antigens.

T-Lymphocytes, or T cells, play a vital role in cell-mediated immunity and help regulate the immune response. However, they do not generate antibodies; instead, they target infected cells and help coordinate the immune response.

questions.explanation: B lymphocytes produce antibodies as part of humoral immunity.

questions.text: Which type of white blood cells are concerned with the release of histamine and the natural anticoagulant heparin?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Eosinophils are a type of white blood cell that is involved in allergic reactions and parasitic infections. They release chemicals that help to kill parasites and neutralize toxins.

Monocytes are the largest type of white blood cells in the human body. They are produced in the bone marrow and circulate in the blood for a few days before moving to tissues, where they mature into macrophages. Macrophages are responsible for phagocytosis, the process of engulfing and destroying foreign invaders such as bacteria, viruses, and parasites. They also produce cytokines, which are signaling molecules that help to coordinate the immune response.
Neutrophils are phagocytic white blood cells that are responsible for destroying bacteria and other foreign invaders. They do not release histamine or heparin.

Basophils are concerned with the release of histamine and the natural anticoagulant heparin. Basophils are a type of white blood cell that is involved in the immune system. They are the least common type of white blood cell, making up only about 0.1% of the total white blood cell count. Histamine is a chemical that is involved in allergic reactions. It is produced by basophils and mast cells.

questions.explanation: Basophils release histamine (inflammation) and heparin (anticoagulant).

questions.text: Humoral response is generated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: B cell or B lymphocyte (bursa-derived cells) is a key player of the adaptive immune response that is responsible for humoral immunity in mammals. B-cell production in humans is a lifelong process that starts in the fetal liver intrauterine and bone marrow after birth. Their development is from hematopoietic stem cells.

Basophils are a type of white blood cell. Basophils are the least common type of granulocyte, representing about 0.5% to 1% of circulating white blood cells. However, they are the largest type of granulocyte and how they work is not fully understood.

T-cells are a type of white blood cell called lymphocytes. They're also called T lymphocytes. Lymphocytes play an essential role in your immune system. Your immune system fights infection-causing pathogens (viruses, bacteria, fungi and parasites) and harmful cells, like cancer cells.

Neutrophils are a type of white blood cell. More specifically, they form the most abundant type of granulocytes and make up 40% to 70% of all white blood cells in humans. They form an essential part of the innate immune system, with their functions varying in different animals.

questions.explanation: In humoral immune response B-lymphocytes proliferate and form plasma clone cell and memory cells. Plasma clone cells produce antibodies which phagocytose an antigen or neutralize toxiss produced by pathogens .
